enrichment.seo_title: QuickMail Pricing and Features 2026: Cold Email and LinkedIn Outreach Tool
enrichment.seo_meta_description: QuickMail is a cold email and LinkedIn outreach platform with unlimited senders, inbox rotation and AI writing tools. Compare plans starting at $49/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuickMail is a cold outreach automation platform built for sales teams, growth marketers and lead generation agencies that need to run email and LinkedIn campaigns at scale. Founded in 2014 by Jeremy Chatelaine and headquartered in Zurich, Switzerland, QuickMail (found at quickmail.com, formerly quickmail.io) positions itself as a consolidated alternative to stitching together separate tools for email sending, deliverability monitoring, domain warm-up and LinkedIn prospecting. The platform's core differentiator is unlimited email senders, unlimited LinkedIn accounts and unlimited users on every paid plan, so pricing does not scale per seat the way most competing cold email tools do. Instead, plans are priced by monthly email volume and uploaded contact list size. As of 2026, QuickMail offers three tiers: Starter at $49/month (5,000 emails per month, 1,000 uploaded contacts, one workspace), Growth at $99/month (100,000 emails per month, 25,000 contacts), and Agency at $299/month (500,000 emails per month, 100,000 contacts, two workspaces with additional workspaces available for $49 each). All plans include a 14-day free trial and no long-term contract is required. Core functionality centers on QuickMail's Inbox Rotation feature, which automatically distributes outbound sends across multiple connected mailboxes to protect sender reputation and avoid spam filters, paired with a free automated email warm-up tool called MailFlow. The platform also includes a browser extension for LinkedIn outreach that lets users build prospect lists using LinkedIn's ICP filtering and run connection-request and messaging sequences alongside email sequences, with replies from both channels consolidated into a single unified inbox. AI-assisted features include a reword-with-AI tool for varying email copy across a campaign to reduce spam-pattern detection, along with automated deliverability insights that flag issues like poor sending reputation or blacklist placement. QuickMail also offers infrastructure services such as domain purchasing and mailbox configuration to help agencies and larger teams spin up cold email infrastructure quickly. Native integrations include HubSpot and Pipedrive CRMs, plus a Zapier connector and, on Growth and Agency plans, API and webhook access for custom workflows. QuickMail has been recognized as a G2 High Performer and has won a G2 Best Estimated ROI award, with user reviews consistently citing ease of use, responsive support and effective deliverability management as strengths, while some reviews note a learning curve around multi-step sequence setup and reporting that is less granular than enterprise sales engagement suites. QuickMail primarily competes with tools like Instantly, Smartlead, Lemlist and Woodpecker in the cold email and sales engagement category, and is best suited for small-to-midsize sales teams, agencies running outreach on behalf of clients, and B2B founders who want to manage high email volume without paying escalating per-user fees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuickMail is a cold outreach platform focused on combining email and LinkedIn prospecting into a single, manageable workflow. Rather than charging per user or per mailbox, the company built its pricing around monthly sending volume, which makes it attractive to teams that connect many mailboxes to spread out sending load without paying extra per seat.

Founded in 2014 and based in Zurich, Switzerland, QuickMail has grown into a tool used by sales teams, agencies and solo founders who need reliable deliverability at scale. The platform emphasizes automated infrastructure management, such as email warm-up and inbox rotation, so users spend less time manually protecting sender reputation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QuickMail's Inbox Rotation feature spreads campaign sends across every connected sender, reducing the volume any single mailbox sends per day and lowering the risk of spam-folder placement. This is paired with MailFlow, a free automated warm-up tool included on every plan that gradually builds sending reputation for new domains and mailboxes.

Beyond email, QuickMail connects to LinkedIn through a browser extension, letting users build target lists with LinkedIn's ICP filters and run connection and messaging sequences in parallel with email sequences. Replies from both channels land in one unified inbox, and an AI rewrite tool helps vary messaging copy across large campaigns to avoid pattern-based spam detection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuickMail offers three paid tiers, all with a 14-day free trial and no contract requirement: Starter at $49/month, Growth at $99/month, and Agency at $299/month. Every tier includes unlimited email senders, unlimited LinkedIn accounts and unlimited users, with the price difference driven by monthly email volume, uploaded contact limits and the number of workspaces included.

Agencies managing multiple client accounts can add extra workspaces to the Agency plan for $49 each, and higher tiers unlock priority support along with deeper integrations such as API and webhook access, which are not available on the Starter plan.

enrichment.seo_title: Quickshare Review 2026: Free Self-Hosted File Sharing Software
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Quickshare is a free, open-source, self-hosted file sharing app built with Go, React and TypeScript. See features, deployment options and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quickshare is a free, open-source, self-hosted file sharing application built by developer ihexxa (George) and hosted on GitHub at github.com/ihexxa/quickshare, with a project site at ihexxa.github.io/quickshare.site. Unlike commercial cloud storage products, Quickshare is software that users download and run on their own hardware, whether a home server, a small NAS, a cloud VPS or a personal computer, giving them full control over where their files live. The project is built with Go on the backend and React with TypeScript on the frontend, and it is released under the LGPL-3.0 open-source license, meaning there is no company, subscription, or paid tier behind it; it is entirely free to use, and revenue or company financial details do not apply since it is a community software project rather than a commercial entity. As of the latest release cycle, the GitHub repository has accumulated roughly 632 stars and 40 forks, with 58 total tagged releases, the most recent being version 0.11.4, indicating ongoing active maintenance. Core functionality covers everyday file management tasks: uploading, downloading, creating, deleting, and moving files and folders; resumable uploads and downloads so large transfers can survive network interruptions; in-browser file and folder management; and fuzzy search across files and folders using multiple keywords. Sharing is a central feature, letting users share individual files or entire directories with others, including anonymous, link-based sharing that does not require the recipient to have an account, plus QR code generation so a shared folder can be opened instantly on a mobile device. For teams or households sharing one instance, Quickshare supports multiple user accounts with role-based permissions distinguishing regular users from administrators, along with per-user upload and download speed limits and per-user storage quotas, which helps prevent any single user from consuming all available bandwidth or disk space on a shared server. The application is cross-platform, running on Linux, macOS and Windows, and it can be deployed either by downloading a pre-built binary from the GitHub releases page or by running it in a container via Docker, which is the most common deployment path referenced in community guides and self-hosting directories like Awesome Docker Compose and LibreSelfHosted. Quickshare also supports streaming uploads, which makes it usable behind content delivery networks or reverse proxies without buffering entire files in memory first, an adaptive UI that adjusts to different screen sizes, internationalization (i18n) support for multiple display languages, and cosmetic customization options like wallpaper backgrounds. Because it has no company behind it, there is no funding history, headquarters, or founding date in the traditional business sense; the "About" identity of the project is its GitHub maintainer and its open-source community. Quickshare is best understood as a lightweight alternative to larger self-hosted file-sharing platforms like Nextcloud or Seafile for users who want a simpler, single-binary tool focused specifically on fast file transfer and sharing between personal devices, rather than a full groupware or office-suite replacement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quickshare is an open-source file sharing tool designed for people who want to move files between their own devices, or share them with others, without routing everything through a third-party cloud provider. It runs as a self-hosted application, meaning the user installs and operates the software themselves on a server, home computer or small cloud instance they control.

Built with Go, React and TypeScript, the project is maintained on GitHub by developer ihexxa and released under the LGPL-3.0 license. Because it is free and open source, there is no vendor, subscription, or company behind it in the traditional sense; instead, ongoing development happens through community contributions and the maintainer's release cycle, with the latest tagged version being v0.11.4.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quickshare covers the core file management operations users expect: uploading, downloading, creating, deleting and moving files and folders, with resumable transfers so large files can recover from interrupted connections rather than restarting from scratch. A fuzzy, multi-keyword search helps users locate files quickly across large folder structures.

Sharing is built around directory-level and file-level links, including anonymous sharing so recipients do not need an account, plus QR code generation for quick mobile access to a shared folder. Multi-user support with role-based permissions, per-user storage quotas and per-user bandwidth limits make the tool practical for households or small teams sharing a single server instance.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quickshare has no pricing plans because it is free, open-source software distributed under the LGPL-3.0 license. Users download the source code or a pre-built binary from GitHub, or pull the Docker image, and run it on infrastructure they already own or pay for separately, such as a home server or a cloud VPS.

Because there is no vendor relationship, there are no subscription tiers, feature paywalls, or usage-based charges from the project itself; the only ongoing cost is whatever hosting infrastructure the user chooses to run it on.

enrichment.seo_title: Quickwit Review 2026: Open-Source Log Search Engine, Now Part of Datadog
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Quickwit is an open-source, Rust-built search engine for logs and traces that runs directly on object storage. Learn about its features and Datadog acquisition.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quickwit is an open-source search and analytics engine purpose-built for querying very large volumes of log and trace data with sub-second latency, using cloud object storage such as Amazon S3, MinIO or Ceph as its primary data layer instead of requiring data to be pre-loaded onto local disks. The company was founded in 2020 by a team of engineers with deep search-technology backgrounds, and was headquartered with a distributed team across San Francisco, Paris, Tokyo and New York. Quickwit raised a $2.6 million seed round in March 2022 led by firstminute Capital and FirstMark. On January 9, 2025, Datadog announced its acquisition of Quickwit, describing the deal as a way to strengthen its log management and observability offerings, particularly for customers in regulated industries such as financial services, insurance and healthcare that need to keep log data resident in specific environments or regions for compliance reasons. As part of the acquisition, Quickwit committed to releasing a major update of its search engine under the more permissive Apache License 2.0 to reassure its open-source community that the technology would remain available outside of Datadog's commercial products. Technically, Quickwit is written in Rust and built on top of the Tantivy full-text search library, using vectorized query processing and SIMD instructions for performance. Its architecture decouples compute from storage, so search nodes can be scaled independently of where the underlying data sits, and indexes can be queried directly from object storage without a separate ingestion step into a proprietary database format. The engine is schemaless, supports horizontal scaling and multi-tenancy through data partitioning, and offers native ingestion support for OpenTelemetry and Jaeger tracing formats, making it well suited for observability pipelines that need to store and search high-cardinality trace and log data cost-effectively. It also supports GDPR-compliant data deletion, REST API access, and both single-node and multi-node deployment models for on-premise or cloud environments. Prior to the acquisition, Quickwit positioned itself as a lower-cost, cloud-native alternative to systems like Elasticsearch for log search use cases, emphasizing that because it searches data in place on object storage rather than requiring expensive replicated local storage, operating costs could be significantly lower for very large log volumes. Customers referenced in its go-to-market materials and acquisition coverage include organizations with large-scale logging needs such as Binance and Mezmo. Since the Datadog acquisition, Quickwit's commercial future is tied to Datadog's product roadmap, with Datadog absorbing the underlying technology into its observability and log management suite, while continuing to support the open-source Quickwit engine itself for self-managed and community use. Organizations evaluating Quickwit today are effectively choosing between running the open-source engine themselves for full control and lower infrastructure cost, or engaging Datadog for a managed, supported version integrated with Datadog's broader observability platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quickwit is a search engine designed specifically for the scale and cost challenges of modern log management, letting teams run sub-second searches directly against data stored in cheap object storage like Amazon S3, rather than requiring that data to be duplicated into expensive, locally-attached search indexes.

Founded in 2020 and built by a team with deep search-technology expertise, Quickwit was acquired by Datadog in January 2025 to strengthen Datadog's log management and observability capabilities, particularly for regulated industries with strict data residency requirements. As part of the deal, Quickwit committed to releasing a major update under the Apache License 2.0 to keep the technology open for its existing community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quickwit is written in Rust and built on the Tantivy search library, using vectorized processing and SIMD instructions for high query throughput. Its architecture separates compute from storage, so search capacity can scale independently of data volume, and it queries object storage directly rather than requiring a separate, costly ingestion pipeline into local disk-based indexes.

The engine is schemaless, horizontally scalable, and supports multi-tenant partitioning, with native ingestion support for OpenTelemetry and Jaeger tracing formats, GDPR-compliant deletion, and a REST API for integration into existing observability pipelines. It can be deployed as a single node or as a multi-node cluster, on-premise or in the cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quickwit itself is free and open source, distributed under the Apache License 2.0, so any organization can download and self-host it without licensing fees. Prior to its acquisition, Quickwit also offered commercial support and a managed cloud option directly.

Following the January 2025 acquisition by Datadog, enterprise and managed support for Quickwit's underlying technology is now accessed through Datadog's commercial products and pricing, while the open-source engine remains separately available for self-managed deployments.

enrichment.seo_title: QuillBot Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: QuillBot is an AI paraphrasing and grammar tool with a free plan and Premium from $8.33/month. See features, pricing plans, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuillBot is an AI-powered writing assistant best known for its paraphrasing tool, which rewrites sentences and paragraphs in different tones and styles while preserving meaning. The company was formed in 2017 and is headquartered in Chicago, Illinois. In September 2021, QuillBot was acquired by Learneo (the parent company formerly known as Course Hero, Inc.), which rebranded publicly as Learneo in 2023 under co-founder Andrew Grauer; QuillBot continues to operate as one of Learneo's flagship products, led by CEO Rohan Gupta, one of its original co-founders alongside David Silin and Anil Jason. QuillBot's core paraphrasing engine offers multiple rewriting modes, such as Standard, Fluency, Formal, Academic, Simple, Creative, Expand and Shorten, letting users adjust output for different audiences and contexts. Around the paraphraser, QuillBot has built a broader suite of writing tools including a grammar and punctuation checker, a plagiarism checker that scans against billions of web pages and academic sources, an AI text summarizer, a citation generator supporting styles like APA, MLA and Chicago, and QuillBot Translate, an AI translation tool supporting more than 50 languages. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Free plan allows a 125-word limit per paraphrase along with basic grammar checking, while QuillBot Premium unlocks unlimited-length paraphrasing, advanced grammar and word-choice suggestions, the plagiarism checker, translation tools and priority support. Premium is priced at $19.95 per month when billed monthly, $39.95 per quarter (about $13.31 per month), or $99.95 per year (about $8.33 per month), representing roughly a 58 percent discount versus paying monthly. QuillBot also offers a Team plan for businesses and schools starting around $7.50 per user per month for groups of 2 to 10 seats billed annually, and offers discounted Premium access to verified students at about $6.25 per month. QuillBot integrates into common writing environments through a Chrome browser extension, a Microsoft Word add-in, and both iOS and Android mobile apps, letting users access paraphrasing and grammar checking without leaving the document or webpage they are working in. The company reports widespread adoption among students, non-native English speakers, content writers, marketers and academic researchers, and has processed billions of paraphrased sentences since launch, according to its own marketing materials. On review platforms such as G2 and Capterra, QuillBot is generally well rated for ease of use and the quality of its paraphrasing suggestions, with common critiques centered on the free plan's restrictive word limits, occasional awkward phrasing in heavily paraphrased academic text, and growing competition from general-purpose AI chat tools like ChatGPT that can perform similar rewriting tasks as part of a broader feature set. QuillBot differentiates itself by focusing specifically on writing refinement workflows, purpose-built UI for paraphrasing and grammar, and academic-oriented features like plagiarism checking and citation generation that general AI chatbots do not offer out of the box.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuillBot is an AI writing assistant centered on paraphrasing, helping users rewrite sentences and paragraphs in different tones while keeping the original meaning intact. Formed in 2017 and based in Chicago, Illinois, QuillBot was acquired in 2021 by Learneo (formerly Course Hero), and continues to operate under its own brand as one of Learneo's core products.

Beyond paraphrasing, QuillBot has expanded into a full writing suite that includes grammar checking, plagiarism detection, text summarization, citation generation and multi-language translation, positioning it as a one-stop tool for students, non-native English speakers, and professional writers who need to refine and polish text quickly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QuillBot's paraphrasing tool offers multiple modes, including Standard, Fluency, Formal, Academic, Simple, Creative, Expand and Shorten, so users can adapt output for casual writing, academic papers, or professional communication. The grammar checker flags punctuation, word choice and sentence structure issues alongside the paraphraser.

Additional tools include a plagiarism checker that scans against billions of web and academic sources, an AI summarizer for condensing long documents, a citation generator supporting APA, MLA and Chicago styles, and QuillBot Translate, which supports more than 50 languages. A Chrome extension, Word add-in, and mobile apps bring these tools into the environments users already write in.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuillBot uses a freemium model: the Free plan limits paraphrasing to 125 words at a time and includes basic grammar checking, while Premium removes the word limit and adds advanced grammar suggestions, the plagiarism checker, translation tools and priority support.

Premium costs $19.95/month billed monthly, $39.95 per quarter (about $13.31/month), or $99.95 per year (about $8.33/month), the best value at roughly 58 percent savings versus monthly billing. A Team plan for 2 to 10 seats starts around $7.50 per user per month billed annually, and verified students receive discounted Premium access.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: QuotaPath is a sales commission tracking and compensation management platform designed to replace error-prone spreadsheets with an automated, auditable system for calculating, tracking and paying out sales commissions. The company was founded in 2018 and is co-headquartered in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania and Austin, Texas. QuotaPath has raised a total of roughly $70.8 million across five funding rounds, according to Crunchbase, including a $21.3 million Series A led by Insight Partners in 2021 that was covered by TechCrunch, with additional backing from investors such as ATX Venture Partners, Harper Ventures, Tribe Capital, Urban Innovation Fund and HubSpot Ventures, the latter reflecting QuotaPath's deep integration with HubSpot's CRM ecosystem. The platform's central use case is connecting to a company's CRM (natively supporting Salesforce and HubSpot among others), spreadsheets, HRIS, accounting platforms and ERP tools to automatically calculate commissions based on closed deals, quota attainment and configurable compensation plan rules, removing the need for manual spreadsheet reconciliation between sales, finance and RevOps teams. QuotaPath has added AI-native capabilities including an AI-Powered Plan Builder that can generate a compensation plan structure from an uploaded strategy document or a text prompt, along with AI-driven benchmarking that compares a company's compensation plans against a database of tens of thousands of other plans, and AI analysis of attainment distribution to help RevOps leaders evaluate whether pay-for-performance alignment is working as intended. Pricing is structured around three tiers, all billed annually: the Growth plan costs $35 per user per month plus a $525 per month platform fee (which covers the first five users), and includes quota management, leaderboards, contests, manager plan views, plan verification, calculated fields, and ledger functionality supporting ASC 606 revenue recognition compliance, along with integrations to six CRMs and three spreadsheet tools. The Premium plan costs $50 per user per month plus an $800 per month platform fee (also covering five users), adding plan modeling, multi-level approval workflows, custom reporting, automated commission-to-payroll syncing (including a direct Rippling integration), API access, single sign-on, and a much broader integration library spanning eleven CRMs, one HRIS, seven accounting platforms and five ERP tools. A third tier, Strategic, is a fully managed, custom-scoped offering aimed at larger or more complex organizations, bundling a dedicated compensation analyst, end-to-end plan design, monthly commission runs, dispute resolution support, scenario modeling and benchmark recommendations, in addition to everything in Premium. QuotaPath also sells an add-on called Atlas, priced from $5,000 per year, though public materials do not fully detail its scope beyond being available on both Growth and Premium tiers. The company counts customers such as Zapier, AirDNA and Keen Decision Systems among its user base, and targets sales operations, RevOps and finance teams at mid-market and enterprise B2B companies that need to manage increasingly complex, multi-tiered commission structures with full audit trails for compliance and payroll purposes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: QuotaPath is a compensation management platform built to automate the design, tracking and payout of sales commissions, replacing manual spreadsheet processes that are prone to errors and disputes between sales reps, managers and finance teams.

Founded in 2018 and co-headquartered in Philadelphia and Austin, QuotaPath has raised roughly $70.8 million in funding, including a $21.3 million Series A led by Insight Partners, and counts HubSpot Ventures among its investors, reflecting its close integration with common CRM platforms used by sales teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: QuotaPath connects directly to CRM systems like Salesforce and HubSpot, along with spreadsheets, HRIS, accounting and ERP tools, to automatically calculate commissions based on closed deals and configurable plan rules. Its AI-Powered Plan Builder can generate a compensation plan structure directly from an uploaded document or a written prompt, and AI benchmarking compares a company's plans against tens of thousands of others in its database.

On the higher tiers, QuotaPath adds multi-level approval workflows, custom reporting, automated payroll syncing through partners like Rippling, API access and single sign-on, giving finance and RevOps teams a fully auditable commission process that supports ASC 606 revenue recognition requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: QuotaPath's pricing combines a per-user rate with a flat monthly platform fee, all billed annually. Growth costs $35 per user per month plus a $525 monthly platform fee covering the first five users, while Premium costs $50 per user per month plus an $800 monthly platform fee, also covering five users.

A third tier, Strategic, offers custom pricing for organizations that want a fully managed commissions service, including a dedicated compensation analyst and monthly commission runs handled by QuotaPath's team rather than configured entirely in-house.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Quso.ai (formerly Vidyo.ai) turns long videos into short social clips with AI. See pricing from $19/month, key features, pros, cons and FAQs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Quso.ai, formerly known as Vidyo.ai, is an AI-powered content repurposing platform that converts long-form videos, podcasts and livestreams into short, vertical, captioned clips designed for platforms like TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts and LinkedIn. The company was co-founded in December 2021 by Vedant Maheshwari and Kushagra Pandya and is headquartered in New York City. According to a Latka case study, Quso.ai reached roughly $3.7 million in annual revenue with a team of about 34 people in 2025, and the company states it has been used by more than four million creators, podcasters and brands worldwide since launch. The core product analyzes an uploaded long-form video or podcast recording and uses AI to identify the most engaging moments, scoring segments by their estimated virality potential so creators do not have to manually scrub through hours of footage to find clip-worthy content. Once moments are identified, the platform automatically generates short clips with word-level animated captions in more than 100 languages, removes filler words and silences, and reformats output into multiple aspect ratios (16:9, 9:16 and 1:1) to fit different platforms from a single source upload. Beyond clipping, Quso.ai includes a text-based AI video editor with a built-in B-roll library, a Brand Kit for maintaining consistent fonts, colors and logos across all generated clips, and a social media scheduler and Content Planner that lets users plan and auto-publish clips to multiple platforms from a single calendar view. Quso.ai also provides a "Viddy" AI assistant on its top-tier plan and social media analytics with AI-generated performance insights to help creators understand which clips are resonating. Pricing follows a freemium, credit-based model, where each credit corresponds to roughly one minute of processed video. The Free plan includes 75 monthly credits with 720p exports, a watermark, and TikTok publishing. Paid tiers include Lite at $19/month billed annually (or $29/month billed monthly) for unlimited 1080p exports, an advanced desktop editor, publishing to six platforms and 10GB of storage; Essential at $26/month billed annually (or $39/month monthly), marketed as the most popular plan, adding AI filler and silence removal, external content support, the Content Planner and 25GB of storage; and Growth at $33/month billed annually (or $49/month monthly), adding a custom Brand Kit, the Viddy AI assistant, unlimited scheduling and analytics, custom branding, priority support and 75GB of storage. Annual billing is roughly 33 percent cheaper per month than monthly billing and doubles the included monthly credits. Quso.ai positions itself as a consolidated alternative to using separate tools such as Opus Clip, Descript, CapCut, Hootsuite, Buffer and Vizard together, targeting content creators, podcasters, business and mindset coaches, video marketers and content agencies that need to turn one long recording into a week's worth of platform-native short-form content.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Quso.ai, previously branded as Vidyo.ai, is an AI content repurposing platform that turns long-form video and audio, such as podcasts, webinars and livestreams, into short, captioned clips ready for social platforms. Co-founded in December 2021 by Vedant Maheshwari and Kushagra Pandya and based in New York City, the company reports serving more than four million creators worldwide.

The platform's central promise is turning one long recording into a full batch of platform-specific short clips automatically, using AI to find the most engaging moments, add animated captions, and reformat video for different aspect ratios, reducing the manual editing time typically required to repurpose long content into short-form clips.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Quso.ai's AI Clips Generator scans uploaded long-form content, identifies high-potential moments and scores them by estimated virality, then produces short clips with word-level animated captions in more than 100 languages and automatic filler-word and silence removal.

The platform also includes a text-based AI video editor with a B-roll library, a Brand Kit for consistent visual branding across clips, and a social media scheduler with a Content Planner that lets users plan and auto-publish clips to multiple platforms, along with AI-powered analytics that surface which clips are performing best.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Quso.ai uses a freemium, credit-based pricing model where each credit represents roughly one minute of processed video. The Free plan includes 75 monthly credits with 720p, watermarked exports and TikTok publishing only.

Paid plans start at Lite for $19/month billed annually (or $29/month monthly) for unlimited 1080p exports and multi-platform publishing, scaling up through Essential at $26/month annually and Growth at $33/month annually, which adds a custom Brand Kit, the Viddy AI assistant, unlimited scheduling and priority support. Annual billing is roughly 33 percent cheaper than monthly and doubles included credits.

enrichment.seo_title: Qwik Framework Review 2026: Features, Resumability and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qwik is an open-source JavaScript framework built for instant-loading apps through resumability. Learn how it works, key features, and how it compares.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qwik is an open-source JavaScript framework designed to solve the slow time-to-interactive problem common in modern single-page applications by introducing a technique called resumability. Rather than shipping a full application bundle to the browser and running a hydration process to attach event handlers to already-rendered HTML, as frameworks like React, Vue and Angular traditionally do, Qwik serializes application state directly into the HTML on the server and resumes execution in the browser exactly where the server left off, only loading the JavaScript needed for a specific user interaction, and only at the moment that interaction happens. This fine-grained lazy loading means a Qwik application can become interactive almost instantly regardless of its overall size, since the browser is not required to download, parse and execute the entire application's JavaScript before a user can click a button or open a menu. Qwik was created by Miško Hevery, the original creator of AngularJS and Angular at Google, along with collaborators Adam Bradley and Manu Almeida, and was first introduced publicly around 2021 through Builder.io, the visual headless CMS company where Hevery serves as CTO. Builder.io itself was founded in 2018 and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and it remains Qwik's primary corporate backer and sponsor, funding core development while the framework is maintained as an open-source project with a broad community of contributors on GitHub under the QwikDev organization. Qwik is released under the permissive MIT License, meaning it is completely free to use, modify and distribute, with no paid tiers, subscriptions or licensing fees tied to the framework itself. Its companion meta-framework, Qwik City, adds file-based routing, layouts, and server-side rendering conventions on top of the core Qwik framework, similar in spirit to how Next.js extends React or SvelteKit extends Svelte. The framework uses familiar JSX syntax and a component model that draws on patterns recognizable to React developers, which is intended to lower the learning curve for teams migrating from other frameworks. Qwik's tooling ecosystem includes first-class integration with Vite for local development and builds, Vitest and Playwright for testing, built-in CSS-in-JS style support, and deployment adapters for platforms including Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, Deno Deploy and traditional Express-based Node servers. A distinguishing internal mechanism, the Qwik Optimizer, automatically breaks application code into small, lazily-loadable chunks at build time and can use real-user monitoring data to prioritize which chunks are most likely to be needed next, further improving perceived performance in production. Because it has no commercial license, Qwik does not have a "pricing model" in the traditional SaaS sense; its ecosystem is supported through Builder.io's continued investment, community contributions, sponsorships, and consulting or training offered informally by community members and Builder.io. Qwik is best suited for teams building content-heavy, performance-sensitive websites such as marketing sites, e-commerce storefronts and publishing platforms where fast time-to-interactive directly affects conversion and SEO outcomes, and it is frequently discussed alongside other performance-focused frameworks like Astro, SolidJS and Fresh in the broader conversation about post-hydration web architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qwik is an open-source JavaScript framework built around a technique called resumability, which lets web applications become interactive almost instantly by avoiding the traditional hydration step that most frameworks require before a page responds to user input. It was created by Miško Hevery, the original creator of AngularJS and Angular, along with Adam Bradley and Manu Almeida, and introduced publicly around 2021.

Qwik is developed and sponsored primarily by Builder.io, a San Francisco-based visual headless CMS company founded in 2018 where Hevery serves as CTO. The framework itself remains free, open source and community-maintained under the MIT License, with Builder.io funding core development and the project maintained on GitHub under the QwikDev organization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Qwik's defining feature is resumability: instead of re-executing application logic in the browser to attach event handlers after server rendering, Qwik serializes state into the HTML itself and resumes exactly where the server left off, loading only the JavaScript needed for a specific interaction at the moment it happens.

The framework uses familiar JSX syntax and a component model similar to React, and pairs with Qwik City, a companion meta-framework that adds file-based routing, layouts and server-side rendering conventions. Its tooling includes Vite for development, Vitest and Playwright for testing, and deployment adapters for Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare, Deno and Express.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qwik has no pricing because it is a free, open-source framework released under the MIT License. There are no paid tiers, subscriptions or licensing fees for using Qwik in any project, commercial or otherwise.

The framework's ongoing development is funded primarily by Builder.io as a corporate sponsor, supplemented by community contributions on GitHub; teams adopting Qwik only incur the standard costs of hosting and deploying their own applications, not costs tied to the framework itself.

features.title: Resumability
features.description: Serializes application state into server-rendered HTML and resumes execution in the browser without a traditional hydration step.

features.title: Fine-Grained Lazy Loading
features.description: Loads only the JavaScript needed for a specific user interaction, at the moment that interaction occurs, rather than the entire app bundle upfront.

features.title: Qwik Optimizer
features.description: Automatically splits application code into small, lazily-loadable chunks at build time, using real-user data to prioritize likely-needed code.

features.title: Qwik City Meta-Framework
features.description: Companion framework adding file-based routing, layouts and server-side rendering conventions on top of core Qwik.

features.title: JSX and React-Familiar Component Model
features.description: Uses JSX syntax and component patterns recognizable to React developers, easing migration for existing teams.

features.title: Vite-Based Tooling
features.description: Built on Vite for development and builds, with Vitest and Playwright integrated for unit and end-to-end testing.

features.title: Multi-Platform Deployment Adapters
features.description: Ships with deployment adapters for Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, Deno Deploy and Express-based Node servers.

features.title: MIT-Licensed Open Source
features.description: Fully free and open source, with no paid tiers or licensing fees for commercial or personal use.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive MIT License
Resumability can dramatically improve time-to-interactive on large, content-heavy sites
Familiar JSX and component syntax lowers the learning curve for React developers
Backed and actively funded by Builder.io, providing continued development investment
Strong performance benefits for SEO-sensitive, content-heavy sites like marketing pages and storefronts
Broad deployment adapter support across major hosting platforms
cons: Smaller ecosystem and community than long-established frameworks like React, Vue or Angular
Resumability is a fundamentally different mental model that requires a learning curve even for experienced developers
Fewer third-party libraries and community components compared to the React ecosystem
Documentation and tooling can lag behind faster-moving mainstream frameworks
Hiring developers with existing Qwik experience is harder given its smaller adoption base
No official managed hosting or paid enterprise support tier from the Qwik project itself

faqs.question: What is Qwik used for?
faqs.answer: Qwik is used to build web applications and websites that need to become interactive almost instantly, particularly content-heavy sites where fast time-to-interactive affects SEO and conversion.

faqs.question: Is Qwik free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Qwik is completely free and open source, released under the MIT License, with no paid tiers or licensing fees.

faqs.question: Who created Qwik?
faqs.answer: Qwik was created by Miško Hevery, the original creator of AngularJS and Angular, along with Adam Bradley and Manu Almeida, and was introduced publicly around 2021.

faqs.question: What company backs Qwik's development?
faqs.answer: Qwik is primarily developed and sponsored by Builder.io, a San Francisco-based visual headless CMS company founded in 2018, where Hevery serves as CTO.

faqs.question: What is resumability in Qwik?
faqs.answer: Resumability is Qwik's core technique of serializing application state into server-rendered HTML and resuming execution in the browser without a traditional hydration step, loading JavaScript only when needed.

faqs.question: Is Qwik similar to React?
faqs.answer: Qwik uses JSX syntax and a component model similar to React, which makes it easier for React developers to learn, though its underlying execution model (resumability) is fundamentally different.

faqs.question: What is Qwik City?
faqs.answer: Qwik City is Qwik's companion meta-framework that adds file-based routing, layouts and server-side rendering conventions, similar to how Next.js extends React.

faqs.question: What platforms can Qwik apps be deployed to?
faqs.answer: Qwik includes deployment adapters for Vercel, Netlify, Cloudflare Pages, Deno Deploy and Express-based Node servers, among others.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Qwilr turns proposals and quotes into interactive web pages with e-signatures and payments. See pricing from $35/user/month, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Qwilr is a proposal and quote management platform that replaces traditional static PDF and Word-based sales documents with interactive, responsive web pages. Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Darlinghurst, Sydney, Australia, Qwilr was built by co-founders including Mark Tanner and Dylan Baskind, and has grown into a company with roughly 100 employees distributed across multiple continents. According to a Latka case study, Qwilr generated about $17.7 million in annual revenue and served roughly 1,000 customers as of 2024. The company reports more than 5,000 customers overall, including notable names such as Thomson Reuters, Drata, Meltwater, Sotheby's International Realty, Yale University and OpenTable, and highlights more than 500 five-star reviews on G2. Qwilr's core product lets sales, marketing and customer success teams build proposals, quotes, statements of work and other client-facing documents using a drag-and-drop, block-based editor, producing output that renders as a branded, mobile-responsive web page rather than a downloadable file. Because documents are hosted as web pages, Qwilr can track detailed buyer engagement analytics, such as how long a recipient spent on each section and when they viewed the document, and can send real-time notifications to the sales rep when a prospect opens or interacts with a proposal. The platform includes QwilrPay, a built-in payment collection feature that lets buyers pay directly from within a proposal or quote, along with legally binding e-signature capabilities so deals can be signed without leaving the document. Higher tiers add automation features such as a Smart Proposal Engine and AI Prefill, which auto-populate proposal content and pricing based on CRM data, along with conditional content blocks that show or hide sections based on buyer selections, reducing the manual work of customizing every document from scratch. Qwilr integrates with CRM platforms including HubSpot, Salesforce, Zoho and Pipedrive to sync deal data and automatically trigger proposal creation from pipeline stages. Pricing is structured in three tiers, all charged per user: Starter costs $35 per user per month billed annually (or $49 per user per month billed monthly) with a minimum of one user, and includes e-signatures, analytics, QwilrPay, core CRM integrations and 10 free automated documents per year, with additional documents billed at $5 each; Growth costs $55 per user per month with a five-user minimum, billed annually only, adding a dedicated Customer Success Manager, deeper automation, custom branding, identity verification and 150 free automated documents; and Scale costs $75 per user per month with a ten-user minimum, also annual-only, adding Salesforce integration, conditional content, the Smart Proposal Engine, AI Prefill, premium design services and 600 free automated documents, with pay-as-you-go documents dropping to $2.50 each on Growth and Scale. QwilrPay transaction fees also decrease as plan tier increases, from 0.25 percent on Starter down to 0.05 percent on Scale. Qwilr reports internal benchmarks claiming customers see a 20 percent or greater increase in win rates, a roughly five-month payback period on their investment, a 20 percent reduction in deal closure time, and a 75 percent reduction in time spent creating sales materials, though these figures come from the company's own marketing and should be treated as vendor-reported claims rather than independently audited statistics. Qwilr competes most directly with proposal and document automation tools like PandaDoc, Proposify and Better Proposals, and it is best suited for B2B sales, agency and customer success teams that want proposals to double as an engagement and analytics channel rather than a one-way static document.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Qwilr reimagines sales proposals, quotes and statements of work as interactive web pages rather than static PDF or Word documents, letting sales and customer-facing teams build branded, responsive documents with a drag-and-drop editor. Founded in 2014 and headquartered in Darlinghurst, Sydney, Australia, the company has grown to roughly 100 employees and more than 5,000 customers.

Because proposals live as web pages instead of downloadable files, Qwilr can track detailed engagement data on how buyers interact with a document, notify reps in real time when a proposal is opened, and let buyers sign and pay directly within the document itself through built-in e-signature and QwilrPay payment features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Qwilr's block-based editor lets teams assemble proposals from reusable content blocks, embedded media and interactive pricing tables, while built-in analytics reveal exactly how long buyers spent on each section and when they viewed a document. QwilrPay enables payment collection directly inside a proposal, and e-signature functionality allows deals to close without leaving the page.

Higher-tier plans add automation through the Smart Proposal Engine and AI Prefill, which populate proposal content and pricing from CRM data, along with conditional content blocks that adapt what a buyer sees based on their selections. Native CRM integrations with HubSpot, Salesforce, Zoho and Pipedrive keep deal data and proposal creation in sync.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Qwilr charges per user across three tiers. Starter costs $35/user/month billed annually (or $49/user/month billed monthly) with a one-user minimum and 10 free automated documents per year, with additional documents at $5 each. Growth costs $55/user/month with a five-user minimum and 150 free documents, and Scale costs $75/user/month with a ten-user minimum and 600 free documents, both billed annually only.

QwilrPay transaction fees also scale down with plan tier, from 0.25 percent on Starter to 0.05 percent on Scale, and pay-as-you-go document pricing drops from $5 to $2.50 per document above the Starter tier.
